title: Own the Outer Loop
body:
I gave the closing keynote at AI Engineer World's Fair. The talk was called "Don't build agents you can't answer for."

It was my attempt to share what I believe agentic engineers will focus on next: designing systems where agents can do real work inside harnesses, loops, software factories, and feedback systems, while engineers still own what reaches users and ultimately the verdict on what is good enough to ship.

There's a lot of talk about loop engineering and building software factories these days, where you run hundreds or thousands of agents to build software. I think what's often missing here is the nuance of how to think about quality and verification, as well as the kinds of codebases where these ideas work best. Evergreen is super different to brownfield.

The core framing for the talk was:

Agents run the inner loop: investigate, implement, test, report.

Engineers own the outer loop: decide, verify, approve, redirect, and own the consequences.

As generation gets cheaper, the bottleneck moves. The scarce thing is no longer code. It is answerability: can we explain the work, verify the evidence, understand the risk, and stand behind the result?

I talked about a few failure modes I think teams will need to watch closely:

Cognitive surrender: when the agent's answer quietly becomes yours before you formed one.

Cognitive debt: when the codebase grows faster than anyone's understanding of it.

Orchestration tax: when running more agents creates more decisions than one human can integrate.

But the talk was ultimately optimistic. Every major wave of abstraction has automated one layer of work and created new work at the next level. I think this wave is similar.

The new work is loop design, evidence design, verification, brownfield stewardship, and building systems where agent work is legible, durable, and worth shipping.

Build the factory. Keep the lights on. Own the verdict.
